name: Scalability, federation and privacy in AML graph ML (BlazingAML high-throughput graph mining; Towards Collaborative AML Among Financial Institutions; FHE privacy-preserving graph ML)
year: 2024-2026 (FHE/Effendi & Chattopadhyay: arXiv 2411.02926, Nov 2024 / SPACE 2024; Collaborative AML/Tian et al.: arXiv 2502.19952, WWW 2025; BlazingAML/Ye et al.: arXiv 2604.12241, Apr 2026)
authors_or_org: BlazingAML: Haojie Ye, Arjun Laxman, Yichao Yuan, Krisztian Flautner, Nishil Talati (University of Michigan; cs.DC). Collaborative AML: Zhihua Tian, Yuan Ding, Wenjie Qu, Xiang Yu, Enchao Gong, Jian Liu, Kui Ren (Zhejiang University / Ant Group-Alipay; WWW 2025). FHE AML: Fabrianne Effendi, Anupam Chattopadhyay (Nanyang Technological University; SPACE 2024).
core_method: Three complementary works on the PRODUCTION and CROSS-INSTITUTION constraints of AML graph ML. (1) BlazingAML (systems/throughput) - a scalable AML system built around a MULTI-STAGE abstraction that decomposes complex laundering schemes into logical stages connected by graph operations, expressing fuzzy (structurally and temporally variable) patterns with unified primitives instead of enumerating every variant; a DOMAIN-SPECIFIC COMPILER lowers high-level pattern descriptions to optimized parallel code for CPU and GPU back-ends, removing the need for analysts to hand-write parallel code. Focus is execution efficiency of graph pattern mining, not a new learning model. (2) Towards Collaborative AML Among Financial Institutions (federation) - the FIRST algorithm enabling AML detection across MULTIPLE institutions while protecting local data privacy/security; combines federated learning with graph-based methods so institutions collaboratively detect cross-institution laundering subgroups without centralizing their transaction graphs. (3) Privacy-Preserving Graph ML with FHE (privacy/crypto) - runs AML inference directly on ENCRYPTED data using Fully Homomorphic Encryption over the Torus (TFHE) via Zama Concrete ML; two pipelines: (a) an FHE-compatible GNN made deployable via quantization and pruning, and (b) a graph-based XGBoost pipeline using IBM's Graph Feature Preprocessor (GFP) to precompute graph features fed to an encrypted-inference tree model.
graph_task_type: BlazingAML: subgraph/pattern (motif) mining/matching over the transaction graph. Collaborative AML: node/subgraph-level illicit-account and cross-institution laundering-subgroup detection. FHE: node-level illicit-transaction/account classification (GNN embeddings + XGBoost on graph features).
temporal_modeling: BlazingAML explicitly targets TEMPORAL fuzziness of laundering patterns - the multi-stage abstraction captures temporal ordering/variation between stages (e.g. timing windows between hops) as part of pattern specification, though via graph-operation primitives rather than a neural temporal module. Collaborative AML and the FHE work treat time mainly as edge/feature information; no dedicated continuous-time/TGN memory reported. [uncertain]
class_imbalance_handling: FHE paper directly confronts imbalance: it evaluates on both a BALANCED and an IMBALANCED AML dataset and reports that graph-based features improve F1 by ~8% on the imbalanced setting; XGBoost + graph features is the imbalance-mitigation lever rather than a special loss. BlazingAML reduces false positives structurally by matching precise multi-stage patterns. Collaborative AML's imbalance handling is not clearly specified in the abstract. [uncertain]
heterophily_handling: Not a primary focus of any of the three. BlazingAML's precise multi-stage pattern matching implicitly resists camouflage by requiring the full structural+temporal stage sequence (not just local neighborhood similarity); the federated and FHE works inherit heterophily behavior from their base GNNs and do not add a dedicated mechanism. [uncertain]
supervision_regime: BlazingAML: rule/pattern-driven graph mining (analyst-specified patterns; unsupervised-style matching rather than trained labels for the mining stage). Collaborative AML: supervised federated graph learning on labeled institutional data. FHE: supervised (GNN + XGBoost trained on labeled AML datasets).
edge_and_direction_support: Yes across all three - directed money-transfer graphs with edge attributes (amount, timestamp) are central. BlazingAML's stages are connected by directed graph operations that exploit direction and edge attributes; Collaborative AML models cross-institution directed transfers; the FHE GFP pipeline derives directed graph features (fan-in/out, temporal amount aggregates) for each node.
typology_coverage: BlazingAML: broad, user-expressible fuzzy multi-stage schemes (layering across multiple hops/stages; structuring; variants captured by the fuzzy abstraction) - the whole point is expressing many typologies with unified primitives. Collaborative AML: CROSS-INSTITUTION laundering where the launderer splits activity across banks (the typology that centralized detectors miss). FHE: generic AML classification, typology-agnostic (whatever the labeled dataset encodes).
chain_coverage: Mostly off-chain / fiat financial transactions and synthetic benchmarks rather than specific blockchains. BlazingAML: IBM AMLworld synthetic bank-transaction datasets. Collaborative AML: Alipay-ECB real-world digital payment/e-commerce-bank transactions (fiat, 200M+ accounts). FHE: standard AML benchmark(s) (balanced + imbalanced) - likely including the Elliptic Bitcoin dataset for the graph pipeline. [uncertain]
datasets_used: BlazingAML: IBM AML datasets (AMLworld synthetic transaction benchmarks). Collaborative AML: Alipay-ECB - a real-world cross-institution transaction graph the authors constructed from Alipay + E-Commerce Bank data, reported as one of the largest available (200M+ accounts, ~300M transactions, both intra- and cross-institution). FHE: a balanced AML dataset and an imbalanced AML dataset (graph-based experiments consistent with Elliptic-style Bitcoin data). [uncertain]
key_metrics: BlazingAML: matches state-of-the-art F1 while delivering 210x (CPU) and 333x (GPU) speedup over prior approaches on IBM AML datasets, with superior scalability. Collaborative AML: effectively identifies cross-institution laundering subgroups on Alipay-ECB; processes millions of transactions in minutes (exact F1/AUC not captured in abstract). FHE: graph-based XGBoost achieves >99% accuracy/F1/precision/recall on the balanced set in BOTH encrypted and unencrypted settings (encryption preserves accuracy); graph-based features add +8% F1 on the imbalanced set; the trade-off is encrypted-inference latency/compute cost. No standardized Elliptic official-split illicit-F1 comparison is reported across the three. [uncertain]
explainability_approach: BlazingAML is intrinsically interpretable at the pattern level: a detection corresponds to a named analyst-specified multi-stage laundering pattern, so hits map directly to a typology (the most compliance-friendly form of explanation). Collaborative AML and the FHE work do not add explicit explainers; their outputs are risk classifications, and FHE encryption arguably reduces transparency of intermediate computation. [uncertain]
explainability_type: BlazingAML: rule-/pattern-(motif-)based, self-explanatory by construction. Collaborative AML: none reported (federated GNN scores). FHE: none reported (encrypted GNN + XGBoost). [uncertain]
code_availability: Not clearly released for any of the three in the abstracts reviewed. BlazingAML: code not mentioned. Collaborative AML: code not specified (Alipay-ECB is proprietary Ant Group data, unlikely to be public). FHE: built on the open Zama Concrete ML and IBM Graph Feature Preprocessor stacks, but no dedicated repo confirmed. [uncertain]
data_availability: Mixed. BlazingAML uses the publicly available IBM AMLworld datasets (Kaggle). Collaborative AML's Alipay-ECB is proprietary and NOT publicly downloadable (real Alipay/bank data). FHE uses standard AML benchmarks that may be public (Elliptic) plus internal balanced/imbalanced variants. [uncertain]
scalability: This is the defining axis. BlazingAML: explicitly high-throughput - 210x/333x speedups (CPU/GPU) via a compiler lowering multi-stage patterns to optimized parallel code; designed for very large transaction graphs and real-time-ish batch mining without manual parallel programming. Collaborative AML: scales to 200M+ accounts / ~300M transactions across institutions, processing millions of transactions in minutes under federation. FHE: the scalability BOTTLENECK - FHE/TFHE inference is orders of magnitude slower and memory-heavy; quantization + pruning make the GNN FHE-feasible but at accuracy/latency trade-offs, so it is practical only for modest per-query workloads, not full-graph real-time screening. [uncertain]
llm_integration: none (all three are classical graph-ML / systems / crypto works; no LLM component).
deployment_maturity: BlazingAML: research system prototype with strong production-orientation (compiler + CPU/GPU back-ends targeting deployability). Collaborative AML: research prototype validated on real industrial-scale data (Ant Group/Alipay), close to production relevance. FHE: academic proof-of-concept demonstrating feasibility, not production-ready due to FHE cost. [uncertain]
integration_difficulty: BlazingAML: medium-high - adopting its multi-stage pattern DSL + compiler is a substantial engineering lift, but the payoff (210-333x) is high for ChainLens's rule-motif layer; conceptually it is a faster engine for exactly ChainLens's hand-coded motif rules. Collaborative AML: high - federated cross-institution setup requires partner institutions and infrastructure ChainLens does not have as a single analytics tool; more a future direction than a near-term integration. FHE: high - TFHE/Concrete ML integration is heavy and slow; only worth it if ChainLens needs encrypted cross-org collaboration.
relevance_to_chainlens: These three define the PRODUCTION-SCALE and CROSS-INSTITUTION constraints ChainLens will eventually hit, and each maps to a concrete ChainLens component. BlazingAML is the most directly actionable: ChainLens already uses rule motifs (smurfing, gather-scatter, peel chains) - BlazingAML shows how to express such patterns as fuzzy multi-stage specs and COMPILE them to CPU/GPU code for 210-333x throughput, which matters because ChainLens targets CPU feasibility and real-time on TRON/USDT graphs; even without adopting the compiler, the multi-stage fuzzy-pattern abstraction is a better design for ChainLens's motif engine than enumerating variants. The Collaborative AML paper is the reference for the cross-institution/cross-VASP laundering typology that single-graph detectors (including ChainLens on one chain) structurally miss, and shows federated graph learning at 200M-account scale is viable - a roadmap item if ChainLens ever federates across data providers. The FHE work tells ChainLens the privacy ceiling: encrypted collaborative AML is feasible (XGBoost on graph features stays >99% under encryption, graph features +8% F1 on imbalanced data) but too slow for full real-time graph screening today, so a graph-feature + tree pipeline (GFP + XGBoost) is a pragmatic, privacy-upgradeable alternative to a heavy end-to-end GNN. Together they argue ChainLens should (a) keep a graph-feature-engineering path (SNA features -> XGBoost) alongside GNNs for speed and future encryptability, and (b) treat scalable pattern compilation as the way to make its rule-motif layer real-time.
key_takeaways: BlazingAML is the standout for ChainLens: reframe hand-coded motif rules as fuzzy MULTI-STAGE patterns and compile them (CPU/GPU) for ~210-333x throughput at the same F1 - directly supports ChainLens's CPU-feasibility/real-time goal on TRON/USDT graphs.

Precise multi-stage pattern matching cuts false positives AND yields free explainability (each hit = a named typology) - the most compliance-friendly explanation, complementing ChainLens's evidence-narrative layer.

Cross-institution laundering (Collaborative AML, Alipay-ECB 200M+ accounts) is a typology single-chain/single-source detectors miss; federated graph learning is proven at scale and is a credible ChainLens roadmap direction if it federates across VASPs/data providers.

FHE (TFHE/Concrete ML) makes encrypted collaborative AML feasible - graph-feature XGBoost keeps >99% accuracy under encryption and +8% F1 from graph features on imbalanced data - but is too slow for full real-time GNN screening; keep a lightweight SNA-features -> XGBoost path as the privacy-upgradeable, low-latency option.

Watch the gaps: Alipay-ECB is proprietary (not reproducible), FHE incurs heavy latency/accuracy trade-offs (quantization/pruning), and none integrate LLMs or ship confirmed open code - so ChainLens should mine the design ideas (multi-stage compilation, graph-feature-first pipelines, federation) rather than expect drop-in artifacts.
